title: Thariq on X: "Lessons from Building Claude Code: Seeing like an Agent " / X

sections.title: Introduction: Agent Action Space Design
key_points: Building agent harness requires constructing action space
Claude acts through Tool Calling with primitives like bash, skills, code execution
Tool design should match agent's abilities - 'see like an agent'
Framework: imagine solving math problem with different tools (paper, calculator, computer)

sections.title: AskUserQuestion Tool Evolution
key_points: Goal: improve Claude's elicitation abilities and reduce friction
Attempt 1: Added questions to ExitPlanTool - caused confusion
Attempt 2: Modified markdown output format - unreliable
Attempt 3: Dedicated AskUserQuestion tool - successful, Claude liked using it

sections.title: Tasks vs Todos: Adapting to Model Capabilities
key_points: Initially used TodoWrite tool with system reminders every 5 turns
As models improved, todo lists became limiting rather than helpful
Replaced with Task Tool for better subagent coordination
Tasks support dependencies, updates across subagents, and modification

sections.title: Search Interface and Context Building
key_points: Evolution from RAG vector database to Grep tool
Claude became better at building its own context vs being given context
Progressive disclosure pattern through Agent Skills
Claude can now do nested search across multiple file layers

sections.title: Progressive Disclosure Example: Guide Agent
key_points: Claude Code has ~20 tools, high bar for adding new ones
Instead of system prompt bloat, used progressive disclosure
Created Claude Code Guide subagent for self-documentation queries
Adds functionality without adding tools to main agent

sections.title: Conclusion: Art vs Science
key_points: Tool design is art, not rigid science
Depends on model, goal, and environment
Requires experimentation, reading outputs, trying new approaches
Key principle: 'see like an agent'

summary.title: Lessons from Building Claude Code: Agent Tool Design Philosophy
takeaways: Agent tool design should match the model's capabilities and evolve as models improve
Progressive disclosure allows adding functionality without tool bloat
Successful tools are ones the model actually understands and likes to use
Context building tools are more effective when agents search rather than receive pre-built context
Tool design is iterative and requires constant experimentation and observation
actionable: Design tools that match your model's specific capabilities
Use progressive disclosure to add functionality without adding tools
Experiment with tool designs and observe model behavior closely
Replace tools that become limiting as model capabilities improve
Consider subagents for specialized tasks rather than adding more tools
Let agents build their own context through search tools rather than providing pre-built context
